event: Austronesian-speaking Lapita peoples voyaging through Island Melanesia reach the Grande Terre and Loyalty Islands, marking the first human settlement of New Caledonia.
Lapita communities establish coastal villages and produce distinctive dentate-stamped pottery, with major sites later identified along the western coast at Foué and elsewhere.
Lapita pottery traditions give way to local post-Lapita ceramic styles as settlements expand inland and across the archipelago.
Ancestors of the Kanak people develop sophisticated horticulture, including elaborate irrigated taro terraces and yam cultivation, supporting growing populations.
Distinct Kanak clan-based societies flourish with a rich oral tradition, the grande case (great chief's hut) architecture, and ceremonial exchange networks across the islands.
British explorer James Cook sights and names New Caledonia during his second Pacific voyage, the first recorded European contact with the Grande Terre.
French navigator Jean-François de La Pérouse explores nearby waters before his expedition vanishes, later found to have wrecked at Vanikoro.
Antoine Bruni d'Entrecasteaux surveys the New Caledonian coastline while searching for the lost La Pérouse expedition.
Sandalwood traders and bêche-de-mer (sea cucumber) gatherers begin frequenting New Caledonia, introducing disease, firearms, and disruptive commerce to Kanak communities.
French Catholic Marist missionaries establish the first mission on the Grande Terre, beginning sustained European missionary activity among the Kanak.
France formally annexes New Caledonia under orders from Napoleon III, with Rear Admiral Febvrier-Despointes taking possession at Balade.
France begins using New Caledonia as a penal colony, deporting tens of thousands of convicts over the following decades.
Discovery of rich nickel deposits launches mining, which becomes the backbone of the colonial economy and reshapes land use.
Communards exiled after the suppression of the Paris Commune, including Louise Michel and Henri Rochefort, are deported to New Caledonia.
The great Kanak revolt led by Chief Ataï erupts against land seizures and colonial abuses, ending in brutal repression and Ataï's death.
France imposes the indigénat code, a harsh legal regime subjecting Kanak people to restrictions, forced labour, and confinement to reservations.
Governor Paul Feillet ends convict transportation and promotes free settlement, expanding European colonization and coffee planting.
A second major Kanak uprising breaks out in the north, again met with severe colonial suppression.
Following France's fall, New Caledonia rallies to Free France and becomes a major United States military base in the Pacific theatre of World War II.
New Caledonia becomes an overseas territory of France, and the indigénat code is abolished, beginning the extension of citizenship to the Kanak.
Kanak people gain full French citizenship and the right to vote, marking a major step toward political participation.
The loi-cadre Defferre grants New Caledonia greater internal autonomy with an elected territorial assembly and government council.
The 'Billon laws' recentralize power to Paris and stimulate a nickel boom that brings heavy immigration, deepening demographic and political tensions.
The Mélanésia 2000 cultural festival, organized by Jean-Marie Tjibaou, revives Kanak cultural identity and galvanizes the independence movement.
Pro-independence parties unite to form the Kanak and Socialist National Liberation Front (FLNKS), launching a period of insurrection known as Les Événements.
The Ouvéa cave hostage crisis ends in a deadly assault by French forces, after which the Matignon Accords broker peace and a ten-year autonomy plan.
Kanak leaders Jean-Marie Tjibaou and Yeiwéné Yeiwéné are assassinated by a militant opposed to the Matignon Accords.
The Nouméa Accord is signed, granting expanded autonomy, recognizing Kanak identity, and scheduling future referendums on independence.
The 'Bercy Accord' secures Kanak participation in a major nickel project, advancing economic rebalancing toward the indigenous-majority Northern Province.
Organic and constitutional laws implement the Nouméa Accord, creating a collegial government, a Customary Senate, and a special restricted electorate.
France redesignates New Caledonia as a sui generis collectivity, a unique constitutional status reflecting its gradual transfer of powers.
The territorial congress adopts identity symbols, with the Kanak flag flown alongside the French flag on public buildings.
In the first independence referendum under the Nouméa Accord, voters reject independence, with 56.7 percent choosing to remain part of France.
A second referendum again rejects independence, but by a narrower margin of 53.3 percent, reflecting growing pro-independence support.
A third and final referendum, boycotted by pro-independence parties amid the COVID-19 pandemic, records an overwhelming vote against independence with very low turnout.
Deadly riots erupt across Nouméa after France advances a constitutional change to expand the electorate, leaving multiple dead and causing massive economic damage.

French and New Caledonian leaders sign the Bougival agreement proposing a 'State of New Caledonia' within France, as debates over the territory's future and climate threats to its reefs and low-lying communities continue.
